title: Lahmacun
subtitle: Lebanese thin flatbread with spiced lamb and tomato
excerpt: Paper-thin yeasted dough spread with a paste of finely minced lamb, onion, tomato, parsley, and warm spices, then baked on a very hot surface until the edges char slightly and the meat is cooked through. Eaten hot, rolled up with a squeeze of lemon and fresh herbs, straight from the oven. Known as Lebanese or Turkish pizza, though it bears no relation to Italian pizza.
sourceNotes: Lahmacun (also spelled lahmajoun or lahm bi ajeen) is a flatbread topped with spiced minced meat, widespread across Lebanon, Syria, Turkey, and Armenia. The name derives from the Arabic 'lahm bi ajeen' (meat with dough). Anissa Helou documents the Lebanese version in 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) as a common street and bakery food. The Turkish version is nearly identical. Both are traditionally baked on a saj or stone oven floor.

content.text: The topping needs to be almost a paste, finely minced or processed, not coarse. A food processor is helpful but not essential; a sharp knife and patience produces the same result. The mixture should be spreadable and relatively dry. Too much moisture from the tomato will make the base soggy; if the mixture looks wet, squeeze the tomato first or reduce it in a pan for a few minutes before mixing with the meat.

The dough is rolled as thin as possible, 2 to 3 mm, almost translucent. This is not the thick, chewy pizza base; it should be crisp and light, charring at the edges in a very hot oven. The topping goes on in a thin, even layer right to the edge. The whole thing bakes in 7 to 8 minutes. Serve hot, squeeze lemon over the top, add fresh parsley and tomato slices, and roll it up to eat.

items.name: Flat-leaf parsley
items.prepNote: finely chopped, plus more to serve

content.text: Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together. Add the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} and 170 ml warm water. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Divide into 8 equal pieces, roll into balls, cover, and rest for 1 hour.

Combine the {{lamb-mince}}, {{onion}}, {{tomato}}, {{tomato-puree}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{allspice}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{chilli-flakes}} if using, and salt. Mix well until uniform, or pulse briefly in a {{food-processor}} for a finer paste. Taste and adjust seasoning.

Heat the oven to 250°C fan with baking trays inside. Roll each dough ball on a lightly floured surface to a very thin circle or oval, about 20 to 22 cm across. Spread a thin, even layer of the meat topping over the whole surface with the back of a spoon. Slide carefully onto the hot trays. Bake for 7 to 8 minutes until the edges are charred and the meat is cooked through. Serve immediately with {{lemon}} wedges and fresh parsley.

items.fix: Deseed and squeeze moisture from the tomato before adding to the mix. The tray must be screaming hot when the lahmacun goes on
items.cause: Topping too wet or tray not pre-heated
items.symptom: Base is soggy under the meat

items.fix: The topping should be spread thin, 3 to 4 mm. At 250°C, the meat cooks in 7 to 8 minutes. If the oven runs cool, add 2 to 3 minutes
items.cause: Oven not hot enough or topping too thick

items.fix: After 1 hour the gluten should be relaxed enough to roll thin. If it keeps springing back, cover and rest for another 20 minutes
Taste the raw topping mixture. It should be well spiced. The allspice and cumin should be prominent. Season assertively since the flavour mellows with cooking

content.text: Lahmacun is one of the foundational street foods of the Levant and Anatolia. In Lebanon it appears in bakeries and street stalls as a breakfast and lunch food, often made to order and eaten fresh. The same dish in Turkey, called lahmacun, is ubiquitous as a fast food: thin, crisp, covered in spiced meat, rolled around herbs and lemon. In Armenia, a similar dish called lahmajoun is a diaspora staple. The overlap between these cuisines reflects the shared Ottoman culinary heritage of the region. The dish is one of those cases where the same thing has been adopted so thoroughly by different cultures that each claims it as their own, and all of them are right.
